---
title: "Methods"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
theme: cosmo
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

## Study population

This cross-sectional study was based on data from the health assessment in the ADDITION-PRO study, an observational cohort study conducted from 2009-2011. The ADDITION-PRO cohort was sampled from the ADDITION-DK study, a population-based high-risk screening program for type 2 diabetes conducted in Danish general practices from 2001-2006 [@lauritzen2000]. To identify the high-risk population, participants in the ADDITION-DK study (n = 163,189) were initially invited to complete a validated diabetes risk score questionnaire which assessed age, sex, body mass index (BMI), known hypertension, physical activity level and family history of type 2 diabetes [@glümer2004]. Individuals identified as high-risk (≥5 points (n = 26,491)) were subsequently invited to participate in a stepwise screening protocol comprising measures of random blood glucose (RBG), glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c), fasting blood glucose (FBG) and oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) [@dalsgaard2010]. Following completion of the screening program, participants without diabetes (n=22,200) were further stratified into five categories based on their glycemic status and diabetes risk: 1) risk score \<5 points, 2) risk score ≥ 5 points with normoglycemia, 3) isolated impaired fasting glucose (iIFG), 4) isolated impaired glucose tolerance (iIGT) and 5) combined impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance. From this stratification, 2,082 individuals agreed to participate in the ADDITION-PRO health assessment from 2009-2011 [@johansen2012]. The present study included participants from all study centers for the clustering analysis to increase the sample size for identifying response patterns. Subsequent regression models examining associations between clusters and measures of fat distribution were restricted to the two study centers where ultrasound measures were performed [**(Copenhagen and Aarhus, n = x).**]{.underline} [**In total, xx participants were included in the clustering analysis and xx in the regression models. Further details of the exclusion of participants are shown in figure x**]{.underline}

The ADDITION-PRO study was approved by the scientific ethics committee in the Central Denmark Region (no: 20000183) and was conducted in accordance with the Helsinki declaration. All participants provided written informed consent prior to their participation in the study.

## Study procedures

### Biochemical measures

Dynamic measures of glucose and insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon were obtained through venous blood samples drawn at 0, 30 and 120 minutes during a standard 2-hour 75g oral glucose tolerance test given to participants after an overnight fast of ≥ 8 hours. Plasma glucose was analyzed using the Hitachi 912 system (Roche Diagnostics, Mannheim, Germany) or the Vitros 5600 Integrated System (Ortho Clinical Diagnostics Illkirch Cedex, France). Subsequently, all values analyzed with Vitros 5600 were converted to Hitachi 912 values. Serum insulin was measured by immunoassay (AutoDELFIA, Perkin Elmer, Massachusetts, United States) [@johansen2012]. For the analysis of GLP-1, GIP and glucagon, blood samples were collected in EDTA-containing tubes and immediately cooled on ice. After centrifugation to obtain plasma, the tubes were kept frozen at -80°C until analysis [@johansen2012]. Total plasma GLP-1 and GIP (including both intact hormones and the metabolites GLP-1 9-36 amide and GIP 3-42) were measured by radioimmunoassay (RIA) as previously described [@holst1983; @orskov1994; @lindgren2011; @møller2016; @færch2015; @færch2016]. For both GLP-1 and GIP, the analytical detection limit was 1 pmol/l with intra- and interassay coefficients of variation of 6% and 15%, respectively. Plasma glucagon was measured using a C-terminal-specific RIA as previously described [@færch2016; @knop2007; @toft-nielsen2001], with an analytical detection limit of 1 pmol/l and intra- and interassay coefficients of variation of 6% and 15%, respectively.

### Measures of body fat distribution

Measures of abdominal fat distribution were assessed by ultrasonography (Logiq9 ultrasound machine, GE Healthcare, Waukesha, WI, USA) in line with previously established protocols [@stolk2001; @rolfe2010; @philipsen2015]. With the participant in the supine position, VAT and SAT were measured with the transducer placed where the xyphoid crosses the waistline. SAT was measured as the distance from the skin to the linea alba with a 9 L (2.5-8.0 MHz) transducer in the transverse position. VAT was defined as the vertical distance from peritoneum to the front edge of the vertebra with a 4 C (1.5-4.5 MHz) transducer in a longitudinal position. Hepatic fat content was quantified through an image of the liver, captured in the supine position with the right arm held above the head using a curved array 4 C transducer (3 MHz).

### Covariates

Smoking status (daily smoker, occasional smoker and never smoked) was obtained through a general health questionnaire sent out to each participant prior to their health assessment. Physical activity was assessed by placing a combined heart rate and accelerometer monitor (ActiHeartW CamNTech, Ltd., Cambridge, United Kingdom[@brage2005]) horizontally on the participants' chest, one at the lower part of the sternum and the other one at the same horizontal level as laterally to the left side as possible. The monitor was set up to record free-living physical activity every 60 seconds and participants were asked to wear the monitor for seven days while maintaining their usual physical activity pattern during the period. Based on the measurements from the monitor, calculations of physical activity energy expenditure (PAEE) were performed using a branched equation model combining minute-to-minute heart rate and accelerometry measures [@brage2004; @johansen2012].

### Calculations

Area under the curve (AUC) for glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon from 0-120 minutes during the OGTT was calculated using the trapezoid rule. For example, AUC-glucose was calculated as:

$$AUC_{glucose} = \frac{glucose_0 + glucose_{30}}{2} \times 30 + \frac{glucose_{30} + glucose_{120}}{2} \times 90$$

As a measure of insulin sensitivity (ISI) Gutt's index was calculated [@gutt2000] as

$$ISI_{0,120} = \frac{75000 + 180(glucose_{t0} - glucose_{t120}) \times 0.19 \times body\ weight}{120 \times \left(\frac{glucose_{t0} + glucose_{t120}}{2}\right) \times \log\left(\frac{insulin_{t0} + insulin_{t120}}{6.945 \times 2}\right)}$$

Glucagon suppression (%) from 0-120 minutes was calculated as

$$Glucagon\ suppression = \left(1 - \frac{glucagon_{120}}{glucagon_{0}}\right) \times 100\%$$

Glucagon suppression (%) for OGTT intervals 0-30 and 30-120 was calculated accordingly.

### Statistical analysis

Characteristics of the study population were stratified by glycemic status and described using medians (25th and 75th quartiles) for continuous variables and frequencies (n, percentages) for categorical variables.

Five variables were selected for clustering to capture the interplay between glucose, insulin, incretin secretion (GLP1 and GIP) and glucagon responses during the total time period of the OGTT (0-120 minutes): AUC-glucose, Gutt's index for insulin sensitivity (ISI), AUC-GLP1, AUC-GIP and glucagon suppression. In line with previous studies using data-driven approaches to identify subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes [@wagner2021; @huemer2025], summarized measures were used to capture the full glucose and hormonal response during the OGTT, while Gutt's index was included as a measure of insulin sensitivity derived from the same time-period.

K-means clustering was utilized to group participants into clusters with maximally different OGTT responses, while minimizing the variation within the clusters [@james2021]. Prior to clustering AUC-glucose, Gutt's ISI, AUC-GLP1 and AUC-GIP were logarithmically transformed to reduce the influence of outliers and right-skewed data. Glucagon suppression was maintained on its original scale to include both suppression (positive values) and rises (negative values) in glucagon concentrations. Furthermore, all five variables were standardized (mean = 0, standard deviation = 1) to ensure that all features have equal impact on the calculations of the Euclidean distance in the k-means algorithm [@wongoutong2024].

Three clusters were identified as the optimal solution based on evaluation of 26 clustering indices using the Nbclust package in R [@charrad2014] in combination with the average silhouette width (0.21) across cluster solutions as in previous studies [@ahlqvist2018; @wagner2021]. The indices primarily combine measures of the variation within and between clusters, and the optimal number of clusters is the one suggested by the majority of the indices. Although the indices and silhouette widths did not agree on a single solution ***(appendix x)***, k=3 was selected as it provided the most balanced cluster sizes and was considered the most biologically interpretable solution.

#### Linear regression models
